title: Plato - Wikiquote
excerpts: **[Plato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plato "w:Plato")** (Πλάτων *Plátōn* ; c. 427 BC – c. 347 BC ) was a [Greek philosopher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "w:Ancient Greek philosophy") from [Athens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Athens "w:Classical Athens") during the [Classical period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Greece "w:Classical Greece") in [Ancient Greece](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greece "w:Ancient Greece") , founder of the [Platonist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Platonist "w:Platonist") school of thought and the [Academy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Platonic_Academy "w:Platonic Academy") (Akademia), the first institution of higher learning in the [Western world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_world "w:Western world") .

title: Plato
excerpts:
**Plato** ( / ˈ p l eɪ t oʊ / *PLAY -toe* ; Greek : Πλάτων , *Plátōn* ; born c. 428–423 BC, died 348/347 BC) was an ancient Greek philosopher of Classical Athens who is most commonly considered the foundational thinker of the Western philosophical tradition . <sup>[[ 1 ]]()</sup> An innovator of the literary dialogue and dialectic forms, Plato influenced all the major areas of theoretical philosophy and practical philosophy , and was the founder of the Academy , a philosophical school in Athens where Plato taught the collection of philosophical theories that would later become known as Platonism .
Plato's most famous contribution is his Theory of Forms (or Ideas) , which aims to solve what is now known as the problem of universals . He was influenced by the pre-Socratic thinkers Pythagoras , Heraclitus , and Parmenides , although much of what is known about them is derived from Plato himself.

title: Plato - Origins of Western Thought - IRSC Libraries - LibGuides

Section Title: Origins of Western Thought: Plato > Plato: LOGIN REQUIRED
Content:
Mason](http://db12.linccweb.org/login?url=http://search.ebscohost.com/login.aspx?direct=true&db=nlebk&AN=924380&site=ehost-live)ISBN: 9781844651733Publication Date: 2010-03-01Aware of the methodological challenges that confront any writer on Plato, Mason handles the issue of Plato's intellectual development and relationship with Socrates with an assured grasp. Thematically structured, the book begins with Plato's principal contribution to metaphysics, the 'Theory of Forms', which forms a necessary background to his thought in many areas. His theory of knowledge, which is intimately linked with the Forms is explored in detail along with Plato's views of the soul, an important theme in itself and an entry point to discussion of his ethics, one of Plato's major concerns.

title: Plato - The Decision Lab
excerpts:
Section Title: Plato > About the Authors > Dr. Sekoul Krastev
Content:
Dr. Sekoul Krastev is a decision scientist and Co-Founder of The Decision Lab, one of the world's leading behavioral science consultancies. His team works with large organizations—Fortune 500 companies, governments, foundations and supernationals—to apply behavioral science and decision theory for social good. He holds a PhD in neuroscience from McGill University and is currently a visiting scholar at NYU. His work has been featured in academic journals as well as in The New York Times, Forbes, and Bloomberg. He is also the author of Intention (Wiley, 2024), a bestselling book on the science of human agency. Before founding The Decision Lab, he worked at the Boston Consulting Group and Google.

title:
Plato's Ideal State: Governance And The Pursuit Of Truth In Politics
							| Architecture  Image Studies
excerpts:
Section Title: Plato's Ideal State: Governance And The Pursuit Of Truth In Politics > Abstract
Content:
Subsequently, he became part of the lineage of prominent Athenian thinkers, such as Socrates, Pythagoras, Parmenides, and Aristotle, who established the foundations of philosophical studies. Until his death, Plato remained Socrates' student and colleague. This paper examines Plato's ideas on politics, the state, and just government, focusing specifically on state organization and its division into social classes, the role of philosophers as leaders, and his reasons for believing that only such a government could ensure justice and well-being for all citizens.

title: Plato | Life, Philosophy, & Works
excerpts:
Section Title: Plato > What did Plato do? > What was Plato’s family like?
Content:
**Plato** (born 428/427 bce , [Athens](https://www.britannica.com/place/Athens) , Greece—died 348/347, Athens) was an ancient [Greek](https://www.britannica.com/place/Greece) [philosopher](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) , student of [Socrates](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Socrates) (c. 470–399 bce ), teacher of [Aristotle](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristotle) (384–322 bce ), and founder of the [Academy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Academy-ancient-academy-Athens-Greece) . He is best known as the author of philosophical works of unparalleled influence and is one of the major figures of [Classical antiquity](https://www.britannica.com/event/Classical-antiquity) .

title: Apeiron Volume 59, Issue 1
excerpts:
Section Title: Volume 59, Issue 1 > Contents
Content:
Unable to retrieve citations for this documentRetrieving citations for document...Open Access Cite thisOrigins of Sex in Plato’s *Timaeus* and *Critias* : Between Nature and ArtificeJanuary 19, 2026Karel TheinPage range: 91-113Abstract Download PDFThis article offers a fresh inquiry into Plato’s views on procreation and into the lack of a clearly defined relation in the dialogues between the biology of procreation and the negative remarks on female virtue, or, rather, vice. The focus is on the Timaeus and the often neglected Critias , with some introductory comments on an important passage from the Laws . The leading question is whether Plato’s account of the origins of sex and procreation implies some sort of female inferiority, which would then translate into the political views on marriage and reproduction.
